mnemonics.title: FRAME ALLOCATION STRATEGIES
mnemonics.content: EQUAL SIZE: EVERY PROCESS GETS SAME - EQUAL EQUAL. PROPORTIONAL SIZE: PROCESS SIZE MATTERS - BIGGER PROCESS GETS BIGGER SHARE. PRIORITY: CRITICAL PROCESSES FIRST - PREFER HIGH PRIORITY.

mnemonics.title: THRASHING SIGNS
mnemonics.content: THRASHING HAS FIVE KEY SIGNS: LOW CPU USAGE, HIGH PFF (PAGE FAULT FREQUENCY), HIGH DISK I/O, SWAPPING IN AND OUT, SLOW RESPONSE TIME.

mnemonics.title: WORKING SET vs PFF
mnemonics.content: WORKING SET = LOOK BACKWARD (RECENTLY USED PAGES). PAGE FAULT FREQUENCY = LOOK FORWARD (ADJUST BASED ON FAULT RATE).

acronyms.usage: A technique that monitors how often a process causes page faults and adjusts frame allocation accordingly. High PFF = allocate more frames, Low PFF = reclaim frames.
The set of pages a process has referenced within a recent time window. The OS ensures enough frames to hold the working set.
Page replacement algorithm that removes the oldest page in memory. Simple but not always optimal.
Page replacement algorithm that evicts the page least recently accessed. Better approximation of optimal replacement.
The processor that executes instructions. Thrashing causes low CPU utilization because processes spend time paging instead of computing.
Hardware that allows I/O devices to transfer data directly to/from memory without CPU intervention, relevant in paging operations.

visualTips.visualization: Draw three boxes representing frame allocation methods: (1) EQUAL: equal-sized portions in each box; (2) PROPORTIONAL: boxes of different sizes based on process size; (3) PRIORITY: boxes sized by importance level with HIGH at top getting largest.
Draw a circular diagram: PROCESS EXECUTES → PAGE FAULT → TRYS TO LOAD → NOT ENOUGH FRAMES → SWAP OUT OTHER PROCESS → OTHER PROCESS PAGE FAULT → THRASHING → CPU IDLE. Show how it forms a loop.
Draw a timeline with a sliding WINDOW. Inside the window show PAGES REFERENCED (working set). As window slides, working set changes size. Frames allocated = window size.
Draw two scenarios: (1) GLOBAL = all processes drawing from one common FRAME POOL; (2) LOCAL = each process has its own designated FRAME SET that cannot be touched by others.

analogies.analogy: Think of a parking lot with 100 spaces (frames) and 5 restaurants (processes). Equal allocation gives each 20 spaces regardless of popularity. Proportional gives more to busier restaurants. Priority gives more to high-end restaurants.

Imagine a student with only one notebook page to work on. Every time they need a new concept, they must erase and rewrite. They spend more time erasing/writing than actually learning. This is like thrashing - too little memory causes constant page swapping.

Think of a chef's cutting board. They only keep ingredients they are currently using (working set) on the board. Ingredients they used an hour ago are put away. The board space represents frames - only needed items stay.
Local allocation is like each student having their own locker - they can only use their locker. Global allocation is like a common cloakroom where anyone can grab any available hanger.
